sources.notes: Hard version of the expected-square palindromic-substring counting task.
Official tutorial link for Codeforces Round 1069.
Independent writeup classifies pairs of intervals and optimizes the second-moment sum.

annotation.summary: Compute the expected square of the number of palindromic substrings in a random color string under the hard constraints.
annotation.constraints: The hard version is too large for direct pair enumeration, so the pair contribution sum must be grouped by overlap structure and parity.
annotation.core_idea: Expand the square into ordered pairs of palindrome indicators. A pair contributes m^{-components} after imposing equality constraints. The hard version classifies disjoint, nested, and crossing interval pairs, then aggregates equal formulas with prefix sums by length/parity instead of rebuilding constraints for each pair.
annotation.complexity: Near-linear or low-polynomial after grouping interval-pair formulas.
tricks: aggregate by parity and interval geometry

solution_variants.name: grouped-second-moment
solution_variants.summary: Group ordered pairs of substring-palindrome events by their interval relation and sum the corresponding probabilities.
solution_variants.complexity: Optimized from direct pair enumeration using prefix sums/grouping

tags.evidence: The answer is E[X^2], expanded into probabilities of ordered pairs of palindrome indicators.
Each pair contribution is computed by counting equality components induced by palindrome constraints.
The hard version needs prefix-sum style aggregation over many interval-pair classes.
tags.description: Use prefix sums or cumulative buckets to aggregate repeated contribution formulas.
tags.created_reason: The database needs a general retrieval tag for non-DP prefix-sum aggregation optimizations.

tags.evidence: The optimization depends on separating interval pairs by disjoint/nested/crossing relationships.
tags.description: Solve an interval-pair problem by classifying how two intervals overlap before summing contributions.
tags.created_reason: Overlap classification is a reusable technique distinct from generic combinatorics.
